name: Validation
summary: Conformance reference for form validation interop. Observable states/events per level (control/group/form/nested), three conformance tiers, DI-replaceable concerns. Implementations from different creators become swappable (L1) and combinable (L2).

name: Error Recovery
summary: Conformance reference for mechanism failure recovery via pluggable handlers. Defines the CustomRecoveryHandler contract, registry resolution pattern, and observable states. Applies to any operation — HTTP, database, computation, async tasks.

name: Anchor Positioning
summary: Single positioning provider that any component delegates to via the CustomPositioner contract. Native-first (CSS Anchor Positioning) with pluggable adapters (Floating UI) so components share one engine instead of bundling many.

name: Typed Event
summary: Typed, self-describing event classes with provider-based scoping. Defines TypedEventClass plus listenForScoped / provider option so cross-team events discriminate via instanceof instead of fragile name matching.

name: Trace Observability
summary: Trace propagation and instrumentation contract aligning with W3C Trace Context and OpenTelemetry. Defines header propagation, optional trace-id meta embedding, and suggested client API shape (trace / effect).

name: Analytics Event Vocabulary
summary: Vendor-neutral analytics instrumentation contract using the Segment Spec vocabulary (Identify, Track, Page). One call site routes to any conformant backend (Segment, Mixpanel, GA4, custom).

name: Change Tracking
summary: Conformance reference for observing state mutation as a normalized Change Record (path/op/old/new/source/timestamp). Defines the CustomChangeStrategy contract, a strategy registry with per-scope selection, and observable change events — so snapshot-diff, Proxy, signals, Immer-patch, JSON Patch, and CRDT strategies become configurable and interoperable in one app. Generalizes the validation change-detection concern.

name: Storage
summary: Conformance reference for durable client persistence of the state an app holds — the storage facet of webstates ratified from #011 (gap #4, Option A: no webpersistence project). Fixes one seam: a CustomStorageStrategy registry tried per-scope, with IndexedDB the native-first default and localStorage the graceful degradation, plus navigator.storage.persist()/estimate() for the persistence class + quota. Mirrors the change-tracking protocol's CustomChangeStrategy shape — the strategy contract is the only lock, so Dexie, RxDB (pluggable RxStorage), PouchDB, or a TanStack persistQueryClient adapter all satisfy the same get/set/delete/keys contract and swap without touching the store. Scope is the durable structured-record store ONLY: the response-cache facet is webresources, the durable outbox/replay is webreliability, and conflict-merge is the change-tracking protocol's sync dimension — each tracked under its own owner. Can later graduate into a webpersistence project only if a real cross-cutting sync-orchestration consumer appears.

name: Localization
summary: Conformance reference for localization interop. Defines the CustomTranslationProvider contract, a provider registry with namespace + fallback-chain resolution, the MF2 message-function shape, and observable locale states — so message catalogs and locale strategies from different vendors are swappable and combinable. Native Intl is the default formatter; libraries plug in behind the registry.

name: Render Strategy
summary: Conformance reference for how a component's element tree updates over time. Defines the CustomRenderStrategy contract (readonly key + mount/update/dispose, RenderInput tagged union keyed by strategy id), a per-scope provider registry (nearest-scope wins), and the four registered strategies (declarative-static, vdom, fine-grained, imperative). Re-render is strategy-owned: the strategy subscribes to its own reactivity source, the host never calls update(). The sibling of Change Tracking: change strategies detect, render strategies commit. Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: Auto-Define Strategy
summary: Conformance reference for how/when a custom element gets registered (customElements.define). Defines the AutoDefineStrategy contract (trigger, optional tag→module resolve, define + scope), an open registry that extends core CustomRegistry, and the idempotent defineElement(tag, ctor) helper plus static tagName as the tag↔class source of truth. The native baseline is explicit registration — the platform has no auto-registration — so every inferring strategy (on-first-use DOM-presence, build-time HTML parse, manifest/import-map, convention-URL, server-driven) is opt-in. The tool carries no default: the default-strategy selection comes from the platform config a project extends, shipped in flavors (strict-explicit, lazy-dom, build-parsed). Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: Changelog Manifest
summary: Conformance reference for a release's machine-readable change description — the dual of a prose CHANGELOG.md. Defines per-module entries (the unit is the module/file, not the package) keyed to a semver severity (major/minor/patch) and a Keep a Changelog / Conventional Commits change type, plus a migration linkage: a breaking entry references the codemod that mechanically applies it, with author + integrity-hash trust metadata so the auto-update pipeline can verify a migration before running it on consumer source. Release severity is derived strictest-wins from the entries, not hand-asserted. Borrows existing vocabulary (semver, Keep a Changelog, Conventional Commits) for the human layer; standardizes only the machine schema + migration linkage (the novel part). Consumed by the auto-update pipeline (#101), upgraders (#094), incremental delivery (#103), and MaaS versioning (#088).

name: App Shell Compatibility
summary: Conformance reference for the flat coexistence manifest each micro-app authors so an app shell can decide — on navigation — whether the next page loads inside the same SPA (compatible) or needs a hard refresh (incompatible). Ruled by #104. The unit is the declared requirement, not the computed chart: a providers block (semver ranges per shared provider, npm-peerDependencies / MF-shared shaped, each singleton-vs-isolatable and mandatory-vs-optional) plus OSGi-Require-Capability-style namespaced claims for the DOM dimension no version-sharing tool models — globals (window.*), tagNames (customElements.define collisions), routes. Singleton providers force a range intersection across all active apps (OSGi uses); isolatable ones get per-app versions via import-map scopes. A second claimant on one namespaced key is a collision, detected separately from a version mismatch. Declare flat; the BCD-style 3-state support matrix and the live map are COMPUTED by the Plateau view (#502), never declared here. Gate strictness is a warn-by-default policy knob (block opt-in), per npm engine-strict / MF strictVersion. Sibling of changelog-manifest; consumed by the app shell runtime resolver and the compatibility map (#502) off one manifest — the MF2 mf-manifest.json one-seam-two-consumers lesson.

name: Update Policy
summary: Conformance reference for an app's declared auto-update policy — the committed, portable config that says when a new dep/standard version is adopted and how it is made safe (the WE analogue of renovate.json / dependabot.yml). The declared policy is the only lock; the orchestration runner that executes it is a swappable Plateau/reliability service (the deferred build), the same protocol-vs-runner split WE already ships in Guard and render-strategy. The gate is one graded axis, not a single mechanic: pre-merge — cool-off buffer (minimumReleaseAge) → confidence/severity classification read from the changelog-manifest's strictest-wins severity (#102) → auto-merge patch/minor-green, human-gate major + visual-diff; post-deploy — staged rollout → metric analysis → auto-rollback on regression. The 'auto low-risk-green / human major+visual' rule is the safe-default flavor a project config extends, not a baked constant. Breaking entries route through the changelog-manifest's migration linkage, run by the upgrader engine (#094); the WE-only edge is Platform-side pre-testing a version against its consumers' suites before release, via the provider-consumer graph (#092). Consumes the changelog-manifest (#102) and upgrader engine (#094); the runner is built in #497.

name: Guard
summary: Conformance reference for guarding a region's lifecycle against a predicate. Fixes one seam — a predicate/policy attached to a region (document/route/modal/element), evaluated at a lifecycle event, resolved by a swappable CustomGuardProvider (default → project override → custom plug) — and nothing else. The provider contract is the only lock: async, server-authoritative (the front-end is a UX mirror, never enforcement), and revocable. Deny-outcomes are NOT unified — each member owns its own family (exit guard → user-mediated confirm; entry guard → hide|redirect|forbid|cloak, where the 403-vs-404 disclosure call lives behind the provider). Same registry+provider pattern as Anchor Positioning but a materially different (async/trust-crossing) contract shape. Members: exit guard (#273) and access control (#178).

name: Credential Management
summary: Conformance reference for acquiring and managing a user's credentials behind one swappable seam — the navigator.credentials dispatcher normalized as a CustomCredentialProvider (default → project override → custom plug), with passkey/WebAuthn, FedCM, digital-credential, and password as families on one request dimension (credentials:[…]). The provider contract is the only lock: async, server-authoritative (the browser only gathers; the server verifies), and revocable — Guard's contract verbatim. Dispatch is 2-D: fan a request by family AND by member. The lifecycle is an OPEN member set, not a flat four-method surface (which would force get-only families to stub create/store): credential-request (get — all families), credential-enrollment (create/store — passkey + password), session-mediation (preventSilentAccess sign-out — origin-global). Default provider = native navigator.credentials passthrough; a mock in-memory conformance provider ships in the first slice so every downstream surface is demoable without an RP server. Ceremony libraries (SimpleWebAuthn/Hanko) are swappable plugs. Identity produces the auth-state predicate Guard's access-gate consumes (#272). Ruled by #496; built in #483.

name: Mock Contract
summary: Portable, interaction-bearing mock/contract schema for the mock & service-proxy dev-service — the standard artifact, with the server as a swappable provider. Anchors the response shape on OpenAPI, then adds a WE behavior layer OpenAPI cannot express: latency, fault injection, first-that-matches conditional responses, a mock|proxy|record response-kind, and auth/fake-OAuth (stateless by default). The artifact carries consumer interactions (Pact/BDCT), not just a shape, so one contract serves two uses — drive a mock AND drift-check the real service in webcases — because schema-validation ≠ contract-verification. Data-defined so it is portable across providers: the protocol is the only lock.

name: Lifecycle
summary: Conformance reference for a domain entity's lifecycle. Fixes one seam — a declarative status set + transition map (each transition: from → to, an optional guard predicate, and the permitted actor/role), resolved by a swappable CustomLifecycleProvider registry — and nothing else. The transition map is the only lock: data-defined and portable, so a workflow engine (XState, a BPM server, a hand-rolled switch) plugs in behind the provider without touching the entity. Two concerns are explicitly NOT owned here: authorization (whether THIS actor may fire a transition is a guard predicate, delegated to the Web Guards CustomGuardProvider — async, server-authoritative, the front-end is a UX mirror) and persistence (the entity's storage is its own concern). Every transition emits an observable lifecycle event carrying { entity, from, to, actor, at } — the composition seam for audit-trail (#357) and reporting. The canonical presentation of the current state + available next transitions is the Status Indicator intent. Members stay open: a state may be terminal, a transition may be automatic (no actor) or guarded.

name: Audit Trail
summary: Conformance reference for a domain entity's immutable operational history. Fixes one seam — a normalized AuditEvent { target, action, actor, at, before?, after?, correlationId? } appended through a swappable CustomAuditProvider registry — and nothing else. Append-only / immutability is the contract guarantee: a provider never mutates or deletes a recorded event. The before/after pair REUSES the Web States ChangeRecord shape (path/op/oldValue/newValue) rather than redefining one, and correlationId links related events or a Web Traces span. The provider is the only lock — an in-memory log, an event-sourced store, or an OSCAL-aligned backend satisfy the same `append()` / `queryByEntity()` / `subscribe?()` contract, so the audit store swaps without touching producers. Composition is the headline: an audit provider subscribes to a Web Lifecycle provider so every lifecycle transition becomes one AuditEvent automatically; permission decisions (Web Guards) and rule evaluations (decision-trace #355) feed the same log. The canonical per-entity timeline rendering + filtering is the Audit Timeline intent. NOT this protocol: Web States change-tracking (reactive, transient mutation observation) and Web Compliance's enforcement-evidence trail (the platform twin — same schema, different subject).

name: Decision Record
summary: Conformance reference for an explainable, reproducible decision. Fixes one seam — the normalized DecisionRecord { subject, ruleSet {id, version}, criteria[], outcome, reasonCodes?, at? } where each DecisionCriterion is { id, label, input {name, value}, operator, threshold, outcome } — and nothing else. This is a SCHEMA-ONLY contract: the record shape IS the lock, there is no provider, because the decision ENGINE is out of scope (that is the proof-of-compliance concern, #093). Reproducibility is the contract rule: a record carries the ruleSet version it ran against, so the explanation replays. Any producer — an underwriting engine, an eligibility/pricing/moderation engine — emits the same record, so consumers interoperate: a DecisionRecord feeds audit-trail (one AuditEvent), Web Reporting (#350) as a source, and Web Compliance (#351) as evidence. The canonical criteria-table + outcome rendering and result announcement is the Decision Trace intent. NOT this protocol: the rule engine + signed-proof machinery (#093), Web Validation (form-field invalidity), and Web Traces (execution spans — a name collision only).

name: Design Tokens (DTCG ↔ CSS)
summary: Conformance reference for the design-token interchange + runtime mapping. The authoring/interchange format is W3C DTCG 2025.10 (adopted, not coined — the one legitimate Protocol here): a vendor-neutral JSON of { $type, $value } nodes with {group.token} aliasing, so tokens are portable across Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio, Figma, Terrazzo. The runtime tier is native CSS: each resolved token compiles to a custom property (--<group>-<token>), typed numerics register via @property, schemes resolve through light-dark()/color-scheme, contrast through prefers-contrast, and derived accent/state scales through relative color syntax (oklch(from …)) + color-mix() — validated against WCAG/APCA per step (the contrast-validation gate; curated scales are a brand-precision override, not the accessibility net). The standard ships a COMPLETE default token set; a consuming project overrides via DTCG `extends` rather than authoring from scratch. The taxonomy is 3-tier (primitive · semantic · component); the SEMANTIC tier is NOT defined here — it is owned by the Web Intents (density, surface, typography, motion, theme-color), whose refs resolve INTO a primitive token. NOT this protocol: the semantic role vocabulary (Web Intents) and the theme-color intent authoring (#010).

name: MaaS Served-Artifact Versioning
summary: Conformance reference for the version identity of a Module-as-a-Service served artifact. Four parts. (1) IDENTITY INVARIANT: a served artifact's version id = a content hash over EVERY byte-determining input — authored definition + transform/compiler version + params (form/target/strategy) + folded provenance; same hash ⇒ same bytes (cache forever), any input moves ⇒ new id. Omitting an input is the one excluded (broken) option — it serves drift. (2) PIN GRAMMAR: a floating-tag → exact-semver → content-hash ladder, each rung resolving to the immutable content-addressed URL (npm's tag→version→tarball shape); a consumer pins at any rung. (3) PROVENANCE HEADER: the producing core/compiler version emitted as an X-MaaS-Producer response header beside X-MaaS-Lossy, and folded INTO the hash (so a compiler bump alone mints a new id). (4) RUNTIME COMPAT-RANGE: the artifact declares an API-contract-version range against its bare-specifier runtime dependency (e.g. @frontierui/jsx-runtime) in metadata — a consumer↔provider seam-contract; 'satisfies' = the runtime's __API_VERSION__ falls in the range. WE defines the contract (identity rule + pin grammar + header + range FORMATS); Frontier UI implements it (#390 — runtime __API_VERSION__ export + opt-in maas-check validator); the serve path now exists (#461) with a machine-readable, language-neutral serve-path IR + OpenAPI projection (#505, blocks/renderers/module-service/servePathIR.ts) as the polyglot source of truth the JS Fetch origin is a reference implementation of, not the definition. Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: Policy Rule (DMN-aligned)
summary: Conformance reference for the rule meta-schema of declarative domain policy — adopting OMG DMN as the vocabulary (standardize the meta-schema, not the rule list). A PolicyRuleSet { id, version, scope?, hitPolicy, inputs[], rules[] } expresses one decision: inputs[] name the data references (resolved against Web Contexts), each rule is a row of { when: inputEntry[], then: outputEntry[] } where an entry is a FEEL-style expression (Web Expressions evaluates it), and hitPolicy (UNIQUE/FIRST/PRIORITY/COLLECT) disambiguates multiple matches — DMN's decision-table model. Versioning is a contract rule: a ruleSet carries its version so a decision replays against the exact rules that produced it (the seam to the Decision Record / proof-of-compliance log). Scope binds a ruleSet per context/tenant. This is the AUTHORING vocabulary only: it does NOT define the proof format (the tamper-evident hash-chained decision log over Web Traces — the proof-of-compliance slice) nor the enforcement seam (the OPA PDP/PEP runtime split). The policy LANGUAGE behind a rule (DMN/FEEL vs Rego vs Cedar) is a downstream impl choice; the meta-schema is what WE fixes. NOT this protocol: Web Validation (input-field invalidity), and the engine + signed-proof machinery (#093 Forks 2–3).

name: Provider–Consumer Graph
summary: Conformance reference for the introspectable dependency graph over WE providers and consumers — the open, self-hostable half of #092 (@webeverything-scoped, 'nothing to lock into'). Two parts. (1) GRAPH-MODEL SCHEMA: a directed multigraph of nodes (project | provider | protocol) and edges of kind 'provides' (a provider implements a protocol at a conformance tier, read from protocols.json) or 'consumes' (a dependency edge, read from Injector.consumptionEdges() — #400); edges carry a confidence ('confirmed' for build-derived, 'potential' until a runtime trace confirms). Consumption is bidirectional by construction (answers both 'what do I depend on' and 'who depends on me' → impact analysis). Build-time auto-derived from WE introspection is the source of truth (not hand-authored like Backstage's catalog-info.yaml); a runtime drift agent is a deferred enrichment (#092 Fork 1). (2) SEAM-CONTRACT: reuses the existing protocol conformance contract (provider declares its tier) plus the NET-NEW consumer-driven projection (consumer declares the dimension/capability subset it requires); compatible ⇔ consumerRequires ⊆ what the provider's tier supplies. NOT this protocol: the cross-repo aggregator, graph UI, and governance workflows (plateau-app licensed product) — this ships the schema + contract only. Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: Report Model
summary: Conformance reference for a normalized report — the single pivot every producer targets and every renderer/adapter agrees on, so reports from different sources render through the same UI. A Report { id, title, sources[], sections[], generatedAt? } where: sources[] are the producing runs (id/name/kind/at); sections[] each carry findings[], scores[], and/or series[]; a Finding { id, severity, title, location?, ruleId, source } is one result keyed to a producing source; severity is an OPEN vocabulary over a named base set (error/warn/info/pass) producers extend (the Decision Record outcome-vocabulary pattern); a Score { id, label, value, max?, unit? } is a roll-up (coverage %, conformance points); a Series { id, label, points[{at,value}], unit? } is a trend/burndown over time. The schema IS the lock — like the Decision Record it is a SCHEMA-ONLY interchange contract (no provider on the model itself); ingest adapters (SARIF · JUnit · coverage → model) and export adapters (model → SARIF/JUnit) are the swappable bridge that earns the protocol — the multi-vendor format-interop story DMN/Decision lack. Renderers (findings table, coverage matrix, trend chart, score card) auto-render from the model the way the /protocols/ and /intents/ catalogs render their registries. NOT this protocol: the adapters themselves (the Web Adapters bridge), the v1 renderers (#432), and producer migration of the check:* reporters (#435) — this slice ships the schema contract only. Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: Push Delivery
summary: Conformance reference for CLOSED-APP push-notification delivery — the server→client transport that survives tab close. Native anchor: the Web Push API (a service-worker PushSubscription against a push service, authenticated with VAPID). The lock is a CustomPushProvider contract — subscribe(options) → PushSubscription, send(target, payload) → delivery receipt, unsubscribe(target) — so the native browser push path is the default and a multi-channel hub (web-push thin transport, or FCM/OneSignal/Novu orchestration) plugs in behind the same seam. Scoped to closed-app push ONLY: the open-app realtime family (WebSocket/SSE/WebTransport/long-poll) is non-fungible (a hard capability cliff — you don't fall back from a live socket to a closed-app push) and gets its own transport home (#455, by-purpose). A consuming project sees the render intents (system-notification, notification-marker), never the transport. The protocol fixes the provider contract + the subscription/payload/receipt shapes; it does NOT own the OS render surface (that's the system-notification render intent) nor permission state (the cross-cutting Permissions API tri-state). Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: Transport Negotiation
summary: Conformance reference for OPEN-APP realtime server→client delivery — the live channel an open app holds (presence, live data, server-pushed updates). The lock is a CustomTransportProvider contract: connect(options) → a Connection that emits messages + lifecycle events (open/message/close/error) and can send(...) where the transport is bidirectional. The provider AUTO-NEGOTIATES the best available channel against a declared preference order — SSE and WebSocket native-first defaults, WebTransport capability-gated (Chrome/Edge only, no Safari — probed, never assumed, graceful degradation when absent), long-poll/poll as the degradation FLOOR that always works. Mirrors Socket.IO / SignalR / Centrifugo transport auto-negotiation — a consuming app declares realtime delivery and the provider resolves the transport; swapping SSE→WebSocket→a hub provider leaves the call sites untouched. Scoped to OPEN-APP delivery ONLY: closed-app push (service-worker wake, survives tab close) is non-fungible and lives in Web Notifications’ push-delivery protocol (#455, by-purpose) — you do not fall back from a live socket to a closed-app push, so the two are separate homes. The protocol fixes the provider contract + the connection/message/negotiation shapes; it does NOT own any UX render (a presence indicator or live-region update is a separate intent). Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: CustomChartRenderer
summary: Conformance reference for the renderer-swap contract of the Web Charts standard — the lock that lets any drawing engine render a Vega-Lite L1 ChartSpec behind one interface. A CustomChartRenderer takes a (semantic) ChartSpec + resolved webtheme tokens and produces output; the NATIVE-FIRST DEFAULT is an SVG renderer that ships with the standard, with pluggable adapters (Vega, Plotly, ECharts) registered as alternative impls — the same impl-swap shape as CustomPositioner / CustomTransportProvider. The spec is the only lock: swapping the SVG default for a Vega adapter leaves the ChartSpec untouched, because the renderer consumes the semantic plane and resolves presentation from theme tokens, never the reverse. Conformance is TIERED: a renderer declares which L-tier of the profile it supports (L1 core required; L2+ selections/transforms/composition additive) and is scored on two axes — semantic fidelity (does it draw what the spec means) and theme application (does it honour webtheme tokens) — plus accessibility as a first-class axis (an a11y description derivable from the spec's encodings + measurement types). The protocol fixes the renderer contract + the tier-declaration + the conformance axes; it does NOT own the chart's semantic schema (that is the Vega-Lite L1 profile) nor any picker/legend UX intent. Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.

name: Editor Engine
summary: Conformance reference for the rich-text editing engine of the Web Editing standard — the swap contract that lets any editing engine (native contenteditable+InputEvent, ProseMirror, Lexical, Slate, Quill) drive an editable surface behind one interface. An editor engine attaches to a host element and exposes the editing surface over a structured-node-tree internal pivot, emitting normalized InputEvent-shaped changes; the NATIVE-FIRST DEFAULT is contenteditable+InputEvent plus the editing-surface capabilities (contenteditable floor, editcontext upgrade — #628), with library adapters registered as alternative impls (the same impl-swap shape as CustomPositioner / CustomChartRenderer). The spec is the only lock: swapping the native default for a ProseMirror adapter leaves the document model untouched, because the engine reads/writes the structured pivot, never a vendor doc format. The protocol fixes the engine contract + the capability negotiation (which editing-surface tier is available); it does NOT own the toolbar/formatting UX (a separate intent, #630) nor the serializer registry (#631). Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent. Graduated from #590 Fork 2 (ratified).
